summary:
Marco Polo's travels (1271–1295 AD) constituted the most famous journey in European medieval history, taking the Venetian merchant Marco Polo from Venice through Central Asia to the court of [[event:2229|Kublai Khan]] in China, where he served for approximately seventeen years before returning by sea through Southeast Asia and the Indian Ocean. His account, 'The Travels of Marco Polo' (Il Milione, c. 1300), dictated to a romance writer while Marco was a prisoner of war in Genoa, became the most widely read secular book in medieval Europe and profoundly shaped Western geographical knowledge and imagination for two centuries.

Marco Polo's account described with astonishing detail the wealth and sophistication of China under the Yuan Dynasty — the cities of Hangzhou and Beijing with their millions of inhabitants, the canal systems, the [[event:2217|paper money]], the coal-burning, the postal relay system, the scale of Chinese commerce. To medieval European readers accustomed to cities of tens of thousands, descriptions of Chinese cities with populations in the millions seemed fantastical, and many contemporaries doubted his accounts (earning him the nickname 'Il Milione' — the million-liar). Modern scholarship has validated most of his major descriptions, though debate continues about whether he personally visited all the places he described or compiled some accounts from other sources. Marco Polo's book directly inspired subsequent explorers, including Christopher Columbus — who annotated his personal copy of the Travels and died believing he had reached the regions Marco had described — making it one of the most consequential travel accounts ever written.
